No. The §IV.B.3 counsel note authorizes the scrivener's-error correction procedurally; per the note itself, it does not adjudicate the underlying funding question. The panel or reconsideration docket retains authority over award determinations.

Yes. Per §IV.B.3, a transcription error in the score-tally entry documented by a contemporaneous, signed panelist bench note may be corrected in-cycle without convening a new panel.
The corresponding score is removed and the average is recomputed on remaining panelists; the alternate panelist may be invited to score on reconsideration.
In order of corrected score, highest first, per §V.A.3. Fontaine (76.0) precedes Okafor-Vance (75.4).

Not determined. Funding is contingent on (a) the reconsideration being granted at the May 11 docket and (b) availability of cycle funds. With $25,000 remaining and Fontaine's higher corrected score (76.0) ranking ahead of hers, if both reconsiderations are granted Okafor-Vance would be placed on the contingent reserve list per §V.A.7 and would receive funding only from returned or declined awards or from the next cycle at the Foundation Director's discretion.
No. Fontaine's reconsideration is also calendared for the May 11, 2026 docket.

The bench notes (38) are authoritative for the corrected score; the official tally (26) was a transcription error. The correction is governed by Bylaws §IV.B.3, which permits in-cycle correction of scrivener's error documented by a contemporaneous, signed panelist bench note.

No. Only one $25,000 award can be funded immediately from the remaining $25,000. Per the May 8 counsel note, awards must be issued in order of corrected score (highest first); Fontaine (76.0) would be funded first; Okafor-Vance (75.4) would be placed on the contingent reserve list per §V.A.7.

No. The §IV.B.3 correction is procedural and does not itself grant the award. The reconsideration docket on May 11 must still grant the appeal, and any award is then contingent on cycle funds and the funding-order rule of §V.A.3.
